title: Adapters: Adding Bottleneck Layers
concept: Adapters insert small, trainable bottleneck layers inside the Transformer blocks, keeping the original weights frozen while learning task-specific projections.

exercises.prompt: What is the main goal of using an adapter in machine learning?

options.text: To customize a model efficiently by adding small, trainable layers.
To delete old memories from the model to save hard drive space.
To connect the AI to physical hardware like wall outlets.

exercises.explain: An adapter is a small, plug-in layer that lets us customize a pre-trained model for new tasks without retraining all of its original weights.

cards.title: Plug and Play AI
cards.body: Imagine you have a giant, complex machine. Rewiring the entire machine to do a new task would take forever and cost a fortune. What if you could just plug in a few tiny adaptors to change its output instantly? That's what we do with AI!

cards.title: What is an Adapter?
cards.body: An Adapter is a small neural network layer inserted inside a Transformer block. It passes the model's data through a 'bottleneck' (compressing and then expanding it) to learn task-specific patterns while leaving the main model weights frozen.

cards.title: The Game Cartridge
cards.body: Think of a vintage game console. Instead of rebuilding the console's internal chips to play a new game, you just plug in a game cartridge. The console (frozen weights) stays untouched, while the cartridge (the adapter bottleneck) does the work to load the new game.

cards.title: Adapters in Action
cards.body: If you want a giant language model to write medical reports, you don't retrain its billions of parameters. Instead, you add small Adapter layers (often less than 1% of the model's size) and train only those layers on medical data.

cards.title: Do We Need to Change Everything?
cards.body: People often think that adapting an AI to a new task requires modifying the entire neural network. In reality, adding tiny bottleneck adapters keeps the original model 100% frozen, saving massive amounts of memory and disk space!

cards.title: Visualizing the Difference
cards.body: Compare traditional fine-tuning, which changes everything, with adapter-based tuning, which only changes the tiny added parts.

right.text: Keeps main weights frozen. Only trains the tiny inserted bottleneck layers.

left.text: Updates all billions of parameters in the model. Requires huge storage for every new task.

exercises.prompt: Why do adapter layers use a 'bottleneck' design?

options.text: To slow down the model on purpose so it doesn't overheat.
To keep the number of new trainable parameters very small and efficient.
To block unwanted data from entering the neural network entirely.
To store a backup copy of the entire dataset.

exercises.explain: Bottleneck layers compress the high-dimensional data into a smaller dimension (bottleneck) and then project it back, which keeps the number of new parameters extremely small.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Using adapters requires you to double the size of your AI model because the bottleneck layers are so massive.
exercises.explain: Adapters are designed to be extremely lightweight, typically adding only a tiny fraction (often less than 1%) of extra parameters to the model.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to describe the status of the main model weights during adapter training.
exercises.sentence: When using adapters, the original weights of the large model are kept completely ______.
exercises.explain: During adapter tuning, we freeze the original model's weights to save computer memory, only updating the tiny new adapter layers we added.

exercises.prompt: Which of these descriptions was generated by an AI explaining 'Adapters'?

options.text: Adapters retrain all original parameters of the pre-trained transformer model using a very high learning rate.
Adapters are special text prompts that you type at the beginning of your query to guide the model's behavior.
Adapters insert small, trainable bottleneck layers inside the frozen transformer blocks to learn task-specific features.

exercises.explain: The first sentence describes standard fine-tuning (modifying everything). The second sentence describes prompt engineering (changing inputs, not layers). The third sentence correctly describes adapters (inserting tiny bottleneck layers while keeping original weights frozen).

exercises.prompt: How do Adapters successfully customize a massive AI model for a new task without using up huge amounts of storage?

options.text: By retraining all the original weights of the model for each new task.
By freezing the original weights and adding small trainable bottleneck layers.
By deleting half of the original layers to speed up training.
By converting the model weights into binary code before training.

exercises.explain: Adapters insert a small bottleneck layer (down-projection, non-linearity, and up-projection) inside each Transformer block, while keeping the main, large model weights completely frozen!
